parentQuestion: Which of the following statements is FALSE? "Damage to the primary (somatic) motor cortex results in the loss of both voluntary muscle control and all reflexive contractions."

scaffolds.q: The motor cortex controls VOLUNTARY (conscious) movements.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! When you decide to move your arm, that command originates in the primary motor cortex.

scaffolds.q: Reflexes are automatic, involuntary responses that don't require the cortex.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The knee-jerk reflex happens at the spinal cord - no cortex needed.

scaffolds.q: Reflex arcs are completed at the spinal cord or brainstem level.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Sensory neuron → interneuron → motor neuron, all within the spinal cord. Cortex not involved.

scaffolds.q: If motor cortex is damaged, voluntary movement is lost on the opposite side.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Contralateral control - right cortex controls left body and vice versa.

scaffolds.q: If motor cortex is damaged, reflexes are PRESERVED (not lost).
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Reflexes don't need the cortex. The spinal circuit remains intact.

scaffolds.q: In upper motor neuron lesions, reflexes often become HYPERactive (not lost).
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The cortex normally INHIBITS reflexes. Lose the cortex → disinhibition → hyperreflexia.

scaffolds.q: Spasticity = exaggerated reflexes due to loss of cortical inhibition.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! After stroke, reflexes become overactive because the inhibitory 'brake' is gone.

scaffolds.q: The statement is FALSE - motor cortex damage does NOT eliminate reflexes.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Voluntary movement is lost, but reflexes are preserved (often hyperactive).

scaffolds.q: The answer is TRUE - this statement IS false. Reflexes survive cortical damage.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The statement claims ALL reflexes are lost - that's wrong. Reflexes are spinal circuits that persist (and become exaggerated) after motor cortex damage.
